description: A word-level particle that precedes names to mark deep personal bonds of friendship, love, or family intimacy. More than casual familiarity, 'ni' acknowledges those rare connections where hearts recognize each other, where formal distance would be a kind of dishonesty.
sound_symbolism: The particle begins with 'n', the nasal that resonates through bone—the most internal, connected consonant, felt in the body rather than just heard. This meets 'i', the close high vowel formed with the smallest space, like intimacy itself requiring nearness. Together, 'ni' creates a sound of closeness: the connecting 'n' bonds speaker to named while the intimate 'i' maintains the tender space of near ones. The sound cannot be shouted—it requires the quiet voice we use with those we hold dear.
grammatical_notes: Occupies Slot 2, appearing before names of close friends, family members, or intimate partners. Expresses emotional closeness rather than social hierarchy. Reciprocal usage is common but not required—one person may use 'ni' while the other uses unmarked forms or different honorifics, reflecting different feelings or relationship stages. Cannot be used sarcastically or ironically—the sound itself resists such distortion. Age-neutral and status-neutral, used purely for emotional marking.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: The particle embodies metta (loving-kindness) in its most personal form—the recognition of deep spiritual friendship. Like the Buddhist concept of kalyana-mittata extended to its most intimate level, 'ni' acknowledges those who share our deepest journey.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: By providing grammatical recognition for intimate bonds, 'ni' validates the importance of personal connection in creating peaceful communities. It makes love speakable in daily language without embarrassment or exaggeration—normalizing the expression of healthy attachment.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Traditional communities understood that some relationships transcend category—the friend who becomes family, the elder who becomes heart-parent. 'Ni' carries this recognition that the deepest bonds often defy simple labels, requiring their own grammatical space.
